authorities.name: California Department of Fish and Wildlife

sources.title: CCR T14 §5.85(a)(1) — Trout, General Statewide Regulations (inland lakes, reservoirs, and ponds)
sources.quotedText: All inland lakes, reservoirs, and ponds, including those on private lands, are open to fishing all year with a five-trout daily bag limit and ten-trout possession limit.

sources.title: CCR T14 §7.40 — Alphabetical List of Hatchery Trout, Hatchery Steelhead, and Salmon Waters with Special Fishing Regulations (Lake Angela not listed)
CCR T14 §7.50 — Alphabetical List of Trout Waters with Special Fishing Regulations (Lake Angela not listed)
2026 California Freshwater Sport Fishing Regulations (Updated 06/01/2026)
sources.quotedText: All inland lakes, reservoirs, and ponds, including those on private lands, are open to fishing all year with a five-trout daily bag limit and 10 trout possession limit.

sources.title: California Fish and Game Code §7145 — Sport Fishing License Requirement
sources.quotedText: Except as otherwise provided in this article, every person 16 years of age or older who takes any fish, reptile, or amphibian for any purpose other than profit shall first obtain a valid license for that purpose...

sources.title: Lake Angela — Donner Summit Public Utility District
sources.quotedText: Using the lake for recreation is not allowed. Impact from recreational use by humans introduces harmful pollutants, which causes algae bloom and dangerous bacteria, including E. Coli, that adds stress to the water treatment process. The District encourages the community to respect this drinking water source and refrain from using the lake for recreation.

sources.title: Water — Donner Summit Public Utility District
sources.quotedText: The District owns and operates its sole water source, Lake Angela, as well as all related facilities including the Lake Angela dam and water delivery infrastructure.

sources.title: Yuba River — Wikipedia
sources.quotedText: The 65.3-mile-long (105.1 km) South Yuba River originates at Lake Angela in Nevada County (California) about three quarters of a mile north of Donner Pass.

parameters.note: Lake Angela is not listed in CCR §7.40(b) or §7.50(b), so it falls under the general statewide lake regulation in §5.85(a)(1). Note: the reservoir is closed to public recreation by its owner, Donner Summit Public Utility District, as the sole drinking-water supply for the area (see special-access regulation below).

regulations.humanSummary: Open all year under the general statewide lake regulation (not listed as a special-regulation water), though public recreational access to this reservoir is separately prohibited by its owner (see special regulation)
regulations.verbatimText: All inland lakes, reservoirs, and ponds, including those on private lands, are open to fishing all year with a five-trout daily bag limit and ten-trout possession limit.

parameters.counting_rule: General statewide trout limit for inland lakes, reservoirs, and ponds: five-trout daily bag limit and ten-trout possession limit.

regulations.humanSummary: 5 trout daily bag limit, 10 trout possession limit
regulations.verbatimText: All inland lakes, reservoirs, and ponds, including those on private lands, are open to fishing all year with a five-trout daily bag limit and ten-trout possession limit.

parameters.note: Anglers 16 years of age or older must have a valid California sport fishing license.

regulations.humanSummary: California sport fishing license required for anglers 16+
regulations.verbatimText: Except as otherwise provided in this article, every person 16 years of age or older who takes any fish, reptile, or amphibian for any purpose other than profit shall first obtain a valid license for that purpose...

parameters.description: Lake Angela is owned and operated by the Donner Summit Public Utility District as its sole drinking-water source (including the Lake Angela dam and delivery infrastructure). The District states recreational use of the lake is not allowed, to protect the water supply from pollutants, algae bloom, and bacterial contamination.

regulations.humanSummary: Lake Angela is a closed municipal drinking-water reservoir; its owner, Donner Summit Public Utility District, states recreational use (including presumably fishing) is not allowed
regulations.verbatimText: Using the lake for recreation is not allowed. Impact from recreational use by humans introduces harmful pollutants, which causes algae bloom and dangerous bacteria, including E. Coli, that adds stress to the water treatment process. The District encourages the community to respect this drinking water source and refrain from using the lake for recreation.
